edges.description: Loss of HIBCH activity blocks valine breakdown at the mitochondrial hydrolysis step.
Valine catabolic impairment secondarily contributes to combined respiratory chain and pyruvate dehydrogenase dysfunction.
HIBCH-related valine catabolic impairment is associated with elevated hydroxy-C4-carnitine in dried blood spots.
The valine catabolic block produces elevated urinary 2,3-dihydroxy-2-methylbutyrate.
Toxic valine-derived intermediates are reflected by elevated urinary S-(2-carboxypropyl)cysteamine.
Mitochondrial energy failure and basal ganglia injury manifest as a Leigh-like neurodegenerative syndrome.
Leigh-like neurodegeneration causes early neurodevelopmental delay.
Progressive mitochondrial encephalopathy can cause loss of previously acquired developmental skills.
Central neurodegeneration contributes to low tone.
Basal ganglia and mitochondrial injury manifest as encephalopathy.
Leigh-like mitochondrial injury produces bilateral basal ganglia lesions, sometimes with brainstem involvement.
Longitudinal neuroimaging can show progressive cerebellar atrophy as part of the expanded Leigh-like HIBCH neuroimaging spectrum.
Neurodegenerative disease in infancy can impair feeding.
Infantile-onset HIBCH deficiency can present with vomiting during the early Leigh-like neurologic disease course.
Progressive HIBCH deficiency can produce a movement disorder in later infancy or childhood.
Leigh-like neurodegeneration can include spasticity requiring standard symptomatic management.
Leigh-like HIBCH disease can present with seizures in neonatal and infantile-onset disease.
Later-onset HIBCH neurodegeneration can include paroxysmal dystonia as part of the movement-disorder phenotype.
Infantile-onset HIBCH deficiency can include microcephaly as part of the neurodevelopmental disease spectrum.
Leigh-like HIBCH neurodegeneration can include visual impairment.
Late-onset HIBCH deficiency can include variable cognitive impairment.
Elevated C4-OH acylcarnitine reports disturbed valine catabolism downstream of impaired HIBCH activity.
Elevated urinary 2,3-dihydroxy-2-methylbutyrate reports accumulation of valine-derived intermediates from the HIBCH catabolic block.
Elevated urinary S-(2-carboxypropyl)cysteamine reports toxic valine-derived intermediate buildup in HIBCH deficiency.
Increased or variably abnormal lactate is a nonspecific readout of the secondary mitochondrial dysfunction associated with HIBCH deficiency.

nodes.description: Brain MRI commonly shows basal ganglia involvement as part of the Leigh/Leigh-like neuroimaging pattern.

evidence.reference_title: Leigh-like syndrome with progressive cerebellar atrophy caused by novel HIBCH variants
evidence.snippet: Brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) shows bilateral lesions in the basal ganglia with/without brainstem involvement.

evidence.reference_title: Delineating the neurological phenotype in children with defects in the ECHS1 or HIBCH gene
evidence.snippet: Basal ganglia lesions (18 patients) were associated with small cysts in the putamen/pallidum in half of the cases, a characteristic hallmark for diagnosis.

evidence.reference_title: Characterization of 3-Hydroxyisobutyryl-Coa Hydrolase (HIBCH) Deficiency in Bahrain: A Retrospective Cohort Study
evidence.snippet: Biochemical profiling revealed elevated C4-OH acylcarnitine, with variable abnormalities in blood lactate, amino acids, and respiratory chain complexes.

readouts.interpretation: Increased or variably abnormal lactate is a nonspecific readout of the secondary mitochondrial dysfunction associated with HIBCH deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Cinical, Metabolic, and Genetic Analysis and Follow-Up of Eight Patients With HIBCH Mutations Presenting With Leigh/Leigh-Like Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Five (5/7) patients presented with elevated C4-OH in dried blood spots, and the level was probably correlated with the NPMDS scores during the peak disease phase.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated C4-OH acylcarnitine reports disturbed valine catabolism downstream of impaired HIBCH activity.

nodes.description: Progressive cerebellar atrophy has been reported on long-term MRI follow-up in genetically confirmed HIBCH deficiency, expanding the recognized neuroimaging spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: Leigh-like syndrome with progressive cerebellar atrophy caused by novel HIBCH variants
evidence.snippet: Long-term follow-up MRI revealed progressive cerebellar atrophy, which expands the phenotypic spectrum of HIBCH deficiency.

nodes.description: Variable cognitive impairment can occur in the late-onset HIBCH phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA Hydrolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Late onset is the second most common phenotype, presenting in childhood as a slowly progressive disease with significant movement disorder with or without paroxysmal dystonia, variable cognitive impairment, and high survivability.

nodes.description: Loss of previously acquired developmental skills is a common early manifestation of HIBCH deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Cinical, Metabolic, and Genetic Analysis and Follow-Up of Eight Patients With HIBCH Mutations Presenting With Leigh/Leigh-Like Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The most prominent clinical manifestations were developmental regression/delay, hypotonia, encephalopathy, and feeding difficulties.

nodes.description: Paroxysmal dystonia can accompany the movement-disorder phenotype in late onset HIBCH deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA Hydrolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Late onset is the second most common phenotype, presenting in childhood as a slowly progressive disease with significant movement disorder with or without paroxysmal dystonia, variable cognitive impairment, and high survivability.

nodes.description: Leigh-like encephalopathy reflects the central nervous system involvement of HIBCH deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Cinical, Metabolic, and Genetic Analysis and Follow-Up of Eight Patients With HIBCH Mutations Presenting With Leigh/Leigh-Like Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The most prominent clinical manifestations were developmental regression/delay, hypotonia, encephalopathy, and feeding difficulties.

nodes.description: Infants with HIBCH deficiency often have poor feeding in the setting of early neurodegenerative disease.

evidence.reference_title: Cinical, Metabolic, and Genetic Analysis and Follow-Up of Eight Patients With HIBCH Mutations Presenting With Leigh/Leigh-Like Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The most prominent clinical manifestations were developmental regression/delay, hypotonia, encephalopathy, and feeding difficulties.

nodes.description: Affected children often present with developmental delay in early life.

evidence.reference_title: Cinical, Metabolic, and Genetic Analysis and Follow-Up of Eight Patients With HIBCH Mutations Presenting With Leigh/Leigh-Like Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The most prominent clinical manifestations were developmental regression/delay, hypotonia, encephalopathy, and feeding difficulties.

evidence.reference_title: HIBCH mutations can cause Leigh-like disease with combined deficiency of multiple mitochondrial respiratory chain enzymes and pyruvate dehydrogenase.
evidence.snippet: HIBCH deficiency, a disorder of valine catabolism, is a novel cause of the multiple mitochondrial dysfunctions syndrome, and should be considered in the differential diagnosis of patients presenting with multiple RC deficiencies and/or pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: HIBCH / 3-hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA hydrolase deficiency (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: HIBCH | HGNC:4908 | 3-hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA hydrolase deficiency | MONDO:0009603 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Biallelic HIBCH variants reduce mitochondrial 3-hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA hydrolase activity and disrupt valine degradation.

evidence.reference_title: HIBCH mutations can cause Leigh-like disease with combined deficiency of multiple mitochondrial respiratory chain enzymes and pyruvate dehydrogenase.
evidence.snippet: HIBCH deficiency, a disorder of valine catabolism, is a novel cause of the multiple mitochondrial dysfunctions syndrome, and should be considered in the differential diagnosis of patients presenting with multiple RC deficiencies and/or pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency.

nodes.description: Low tone is a prominent early neurologic feature in HIBCH deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Cinical, Metabolic, and Genetic Analysis and Follow-Up of Eight Patients With HIBCH Mutations Presenting With Leigh/Leigh-Like Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The most prominent clinical manifestations were developmental regression/delay, hypotonia, encephalopathy, and feeding difficulties.

nodes.description: The combined energy failure produces a progressive Leigh-like neurodegenerative encephalopathy with early basal ganglia involvement.

evidence.reference_title: Cinical, Metabolic, and Genetic Analysis and Follow-Up of Eight Patients With HIBCH Mutations Presenting With Leigh/Leigh-Like Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: HIBCH deficiency, leading to Leigh/Leigh-like disease. To date, few case series

evidence.reference_title: HIBCH mutations can cause Leigh-like disease with combined deficiency of multiple mitochondrial respiratory chain enzymes and pyruvate dehydrogenase.
evidence.snippet: Two brothers born to distantly related Pakistani parents presenting in early infancy with a progressive neurodegenerative disorder, associated with basal ganglia changes on brain magnetic resonance imaging, were investigated for suspected Leigh-like mitochondrial disease.

nodes.description: Microcephaly is reported in infantile-onset HIBCH deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA Hydrolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Infantile onset is the most common phenotype, presenting in the first two years of life with feeding difficulties, vomiting, developmental delay with regression, hypotonia, seizures, movement disorder, microcephaly, vision impairment, and episodes of neurologic deterioration.

nodes.description: Later-onset HIBCH deficiency can present with a progressive movement disorder, sometimes with paroxysmal dystonia.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA Hydrolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Late onset is the second most common phenotype, presenting in childhood as a slowly progressive disease with significant movement disorder with or without paroxysmal dystonia, variable cognitive impairment, and high survivability.

nodes.description: HIBCH deficiency can produce combined mitochondrial respiratory chain enzyme deficiency and pyruvate dehydrogenase complex dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: HIBCH mutations can cause Leigh-like disease with combined deficiency of multiple mitochondrial respiratory chain enzymes and pyruvate dehydrogenase.
evidence.snippet: The index case had deficiencies of multiple RC enzymes and PDHc in skeletal muscle and fibroblasts respectively, but these were normal in his younger brother.

nodes.description: Seizures occur in neonatal and infantile-onset HIBCH deficiency and may accompany Leigh-like neurodegeneration.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA Hydrolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Neonatal onset, the least frequent phenotype, is characterized by hypotonia, seizures, and feeding difficulties at birth.

nodes.description: Spasticity can require standard symptomatic management as part of the HIBCH neurologic care plan.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA Hydrolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: standard treatments for spasticity and epilepsy; treatment of movement disorder

evidence.reference_title: Cinical, Metabolic, and Genetic Analysis and Follow-Up of Eight Patients With HIBCH Mutations Presenting With Leigh/Leigh-Like Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: 2,3-Dihydroxy-2-methylbutyrate in urine was elevated in six (6/7) patients and elevated S-(2-caboxypropyl)cysteamine in urine was found in three patients (3/3).

readouts.interpretation: Elevated urinary 2,3-dihydroxy-2-methylbutyrate reports accumulation of valine-derived intermediates from the HIBCH catabolic block.

evidence.reference_title: Cinical, Metabolic, and Genetic Analysis and Follow-Up of Eight Patients With HIBCH Mutations Presenting With Leigh/Leigh-Like Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: 2,3-Dihydroxy-2-methylbutyrate in urine was elevated in six (6/7) patients and elevated S-(2-caboxypropyl)cysteamine in urine was found in three patients (3/3).

readouts.interpretation: Elevated urinary S-(2-carboxypropyl)cysteamine reports toxic valine-derived intermediate buildup in HIBCH deficiency.

nodes.description: Loss of HIBCH activity blocks valine degradation and promotes accumulation of upstream toxic valine-derived intermediates.

evidence.reference_title: HIBCH mutations can cause Leigh-like disease with combined deficiency of multiple mitochondrial respiratory chain enzymes and pyruvate dehydrogenase.
evidence.snippet: Deficiency of 3-hydroxy-isobutyryl-CoA hydrolase (HIBCH) caused by HIBCH mutations is a rare cerebral organic aciduria caused by disturbance of valine catabolism.

nodes.description: Dietary restriction of valine is the primary targeted therapy for HIBCH deficiency, addressing the block in valine catabolism. Special medical food formulas can be delivered orally or by gastrostomy tube, with total protein restriction as an alternative when formula adherence is poor.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA Hydrolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: MANAGEMENT: Targeted therapy: Valine-restricted diet. As seen in other metabolic disorders, treatment using special formulas (medical food) can be implemented successfully via oral route in individuals diagnosed within the first few months of life.

nodes.description: Visual impairment is reported in infantile-onset HIBCH deficiency and is managed with ophthalmology assessment and low vision services.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA Hydrolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Infantile onset is the most common phenotype, presenting in the first two years of life with feeding difficulties, vomiting, developmental delay with regression, hypotonia, seizures, movement disorder, microcephaly, vision impairment, and episodes of neurologic deterioration.

nodes.description: Vomiting is a feature of infantile-onset HIBCH deficiency, the most common disease subtype.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxyisobutyryl-CoA Hydrolase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Infantile onset is the most common phenotype, presenting in the first two years of life with feeding difficulties, vomiting, developmental delay with regression, hypotonia, seizures, movement disorder, microcephaly, vision impairment, and episodes of neurologic deterioration.
